coredata.dc:title: Constructing the interface of g-C3N4/epoxy composites using carbon quantum dots to achieve self-repairing, early corrosion monitoring and superior anticorrosion performance

coredata.dc:description: Based on high barrier performance, the combination of active/passive self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring functions becomes an ideal anti-corrosion strategy. Abundant surface polar functional groups of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) made of juice helped graphite carbon nitride(g-C3N4) compatible with epoxy resin, and effectively improved the barrier property of CQDs@g-C3N4 composites in coatings. In addition, the adsorption of CQDs on metal is the source of the active protective performance of the coating. The fluorescence characteristics of CQDs made it realizable to detect the micro-cracks in coatings. This work provides a meaningful strategy for the design of intelligent protective materials.

head.ce:title: Constructing the interface of g-C3N4/epoxy composites using carbon quantum dots to achieve self-repairing, early corrosion monitoring and superior anticorrosion performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on high barrier performance, the combination of active/passive self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring functions becomes an ideal anti-corrosion strategy. Abundant surface polar functional groups of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) made of juice helped graphite carbon nitride(g-C3N4) compatible with epoxy resin, and effectively improved the barrier property of CQDs@g-C3N4 composites in coatings. In addition, the adsorption of CQDs on metal is the source of the active protective performance of the coating. The fluorescence characteristics of CQDs made it realizable to detect the micro-cracks in coatings. This work provides a meaningful strategy for the design of intelligent protective materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion is a serious problem and can cause harmful effects on infrastructure safety facilities [1]. Organic coatings have been considered as the most effective and economical strategy for preventing direct contact of corrosive species with metal surfaces to avoid metal corrosion [2]. As a kind of organic coating, the epoxy resin coating is widely used due to its outstanding adhesion force, extremely favorable chemical stability, and high-blooded mechanical properties [3,4].

Due to environmental factors (including temperature, pollution, wear and photochemical effects), the polymer coating is often prone to crack in the construction process, forming fine cracks and deep crack [5]. Deep cracks generally cause complete damage to the coating. Fine crack refers to thin, shallow, and slight crack on the surface of the organic coating, which is often not predicted by the naked eye. If not detected and repaired in time, the hidden and fine crack will expand the territory quickly, eventually lead to the failure and the catastrophic consequence of the coating system [6,7]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a multi-functional coating with efficient anti-corrosion, self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring properties to achieve long lifespan protection for the metal substrate. An excellent corrosion resistance system should own three characteristics: excellent impermeability to prevent corrosion of the metal facilities, early corrosion monitoring performance to survey damaged coating part and local corrosion areas, self-repairing ability to effectively hinder occurrence of metal corrosion and enhance the protective performance of the coating.

As the most basic feature of impermeable coatings, its barrier properties play a key role in its protective effect. Currently, functionalized nanofillers with a high aspect ratio endow polymer coatings multiple properties while also improving the intrinsic properties of the coating, such as mechanical and shielding properties [8,9]. Graphene has a high aspect ratio and excellent impermeability [10]. However, in practical applications, the graphene nanosheets are easy to agglomerate due to the action of van der Waals force between layers, which affects the intrinsic protection characteristics of the coating. More importantly, the high electrical conductivity of graphene raises a controversy about whether it can actually be used as an effective additive. Graphene nanosheets can accelerate the corrosion process by forming a conductive path in the polymer matrix [11,12]. The g-C3N4 has a two-dimensional lamella structure similar to graphene, has high chemical stability and barrier properties and, most importantly, low conductivity, so it is a promising alternative to graphene in the field of anticorrosion [13]. Furthermore, the surface of g-C3N4 is distributed with amino functional groups, which make it have better bonding performance with the polymer resin. Yan et al. [14] added g-C3N4 nanosheets treated with dilute sulfuric acid into epoxy resin matrix, and prepared a series of composite coatings, which showed that the corrosion resistance of the modified g-C3N4 coating was greatly improved. However, the addition of two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets into the epoxy coating just simply provides a better passivation barrier performance, which mainly targets the postponed penetration process of corrosion species. When the corrosive species has reached the metal substrate, the coating will no longer provide effective protection.

For local corrosion of polymer coating, early corrosion monitoring can report local corrosion and foresee the location of damage. Currently, early corrosion monitoring of the local corrosion region on the coating, which usually introduces some chromogenic compounds into the polymer matrix, produces obvious color or fluorescence changes between defects and coating, to indicate the generation of polymer material defects and inform the development prediction of corrosion [15]. While self-repairing can renovate damage, thus enhancing the protective performance and the service life of the coating [12]. For self-repairing coatings, it divides into two type of intrinsic actively self-repairing and external passively self-repairing [16,17]. The intrinsic actively self-repairing coating refers to the self-repairing material that restores the original state of the material through the reconstruction of dynamic keys after the material destruction, and is a self-repairing material designed from the structural characteristics of the material matrix itself. It has the advantages of high repair performance, fast response speed, the coating is also sensitive to a large range of damage [18]. The external passively self-repairing coatings are more suitable for repairing fine cracks, mainly refers to the self-repairing function by introducing functional filler in the polymer matrix. When the coating is cracked by the external force during the long time of service, the self-repairing material can quickly locate and detect the crack position and repair the crack defects, realizing the long-term anti-corrosion process of metal materials [19]. As a whole, organic anti-corrosion coating should consider its comprehensive performance in practical application. Self-repairing or corrosion monitoring materials with single function still have certain limitations in the actual use process [15,20].

The surface of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is rich in functional groups, such as carboxy (-carboxy), amino (-amino) and hydroxyl (-OH) [21]. At the same time, low cost, environmental protection, good water solubility and superior optical properties make CQDs attract high attention in the field of intelligent materials [22,23]. Ren et al. [24] used CQDs as a corrosion inhibitor to study their corrosion inhibition performance on carbon steel in a strong acidic solution, which was proved that CQDs can chemisorb on the surface of carbon steel, forming a passivation layer to inhibit the corrosion reaction. Furthermore, Zhu et al. [25] proposed a new concept that introduces carbon dots with a size of 5 nanometers into the polymer matrix, which not only initiates self-repairing properties of polymer matrix itself, but also improves its corrosion protection capability. The corrosion protection capability was significantly enhanced due to the connection and interaction between adjacent CQDs or between CQDs and polymer chains lead to self-healing of local pores/cracks, which reduces the formation and prevents the expansion of micropores/capillaries, effectively obstructs crossing oxygen and water through defects paths. Moreover, the fluorescence characteristics of CQDs are also a concern of current researchers, and the influence of CQDs on their fluorescence properties in the polymer matrix will provide an opportunity to prepare self-monitoring coatings [26–28]. Qiang et al. [29] proposed CQDs and polyurethane (WPU) mixed modification reagent to construct CQDs-WPU modified coating, which showed photoluminescence performance under ultraviolet light. The photoluminescence intensity of CQDs-WPU coated was enhanced with increasing content of CQDs. Currently, it is still difficult to achieve and rarely reported for the smart coating, which possess simultaneously quadruple function with strong barrier, external passively self-repairing, intrinsic actively self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring [30].

In this work, an intelligent protective coating with strong barrier, external passively self-repairing, intrinsic actively self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring properties is developed by finely constructing CQDs@CNNs hybrids. Firstly, CQDs with rich functional groups were synthesized from pure natural orange juice. Subsequently, a high content of CQDs was loaded on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets by hydrothermal method. CQDs@CNNs hybrids, which play a role of one stone killing four birds: (i) improve the degree of cross-linking between g-C3N4 and epoxy, and endow the coating with high-performance anti-corrosion function; (ii) chemisorb the passivation layer on the defective surface of the coating to hinder corrosion reaction for external passively self-repairing; (iii) set off the capability of intrinsic actively self-repairing for epoxy resin coatings, because of the interactions between CQDs and polymer matrix; (iiii) and can also be used to early monitor the microcracks that cannot be observed by the naked eye through fluorescence characteristics under ultraviolet light, to realize the early corrosion monitoring of the damaged part of the coating.

All chemical reagents were used analytical reagent. Ethanol (C2H6O), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), acetone (C3H6O) were obtained from Aladdin Industries. g-C3N4 was obtained from Berlingway corporation. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents. Aqueous epoxy resin (MU-618) and curing agent (CU-600) were purchased from Shanghai Run Carbon New Materials Technology Company.

CQDs were synthesized based on previous methods [31]. 40 mL of fresh orange juice and 30 mL of ethanol were ultrasonically blend in 100 mL of beaker for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture solution was transferred to the Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in an oven (150 ℃) for 150 min. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, then the solution was poured into a clean separating funnel. A certain amount of dichloromethane was added into the separating funnel, which was shook upside down repeatedly to remove the organic substances in the mixed solution by extraction. The separating funnel was quietly placed until the solution was separated into two layers, removing the top piston to release the CH2Cl2 liquid. The upper liquid was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 13 min. After removing the sediment, the supernatant was retained, added into a certain amount of acetone, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the sediment at the bottom of the centrifuge tube was dried in an oven (60 °C) for 12 h to obtain a yellowish-brown solid. The product was dissolved in deionized water and purified through a dialysis bag (membrane cutoff: 3500 kDa) to remove larger sized particles.

Typically, g-C3N4 bulk was processed by strong alkali stripping to achieve g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNNs) [32]. 50 mg of g-C3N4 nanosheets was added into 50 mL of CQDs aqueous dispersion (5 mg/mL), followed by ultrasonication for 0.5 h. Then, the dispersion was moved into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in a 120 ℃ oven for 2 h. We removed it from the oven after the stainless-steel autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution in the Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave was poured into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 7 min to collect the sediment in the lower layer of the centrifuge tube. This product was frozen-dried for 24 h to get the CQDs@CNNs hybrids. Fig. 1.

A certain number of CQDs@CNNs hybrids was dispersed to 13.3 g of epoxy emulsion and ultrasonically treated for 2 h, then stirred for 4 h at room temperature to acquire a homogeneous dispersion system. Subsequently, 6.7 g of curing agent was added into the epoxy emulsion dispersion system and mechanically stirred for 15 min, which was placed in a vacuum drying oven to remove air bubbles for about 10 min. The viscous resin was coated on the surface of Q235 electrode with a coating rod (40 µm), then dried at room temperature for 72 h and was named CQCN0.25/EP. Using the same method, we prepared pure EP, CNNs/EP and CQCN0.5/EP respectively shown as Table 1.

The structure of CQDs@CNNs was characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany). The chemical composition of CQDs and CQDs@CNNs was determined by Fourier Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Britain). The morphology and thickness of CQDs@CNNs hybrids were observed by scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, HitachiS-4800, Japan), transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200x, Czekh) and scanning probe microscope (SPM, Dimension 3100, USA). The cross-sectional morphology of the composite coating was observed by FE-SEM. In addition, the adhesion strength of the composite coating was measured by positest AT-M adhesion tester (Posi Test AT-M). The surface roughness of the Q235 steel was tested through the laser confocal technology (Zeiss, LSM700, Germany). The coating was quantitatively tested by PL spectroscopy (FL3–111) to explore the fluorescence intensity of the coating, so as to detect the self-monitoring performance of the coating. Fluorescence maps of coatings were shown by biotype laser confocal maps (LEICA, TCS SP5, Germany).

Based on traditional three-electrode system, the corrosion resistance of the coating was evaluated on the electrochemical workstation (Modulab XM, USA). The working electrode was Q235 steel with coating, the counter electrode is platinum sheet, and the reference electrode is saturated calomel electrode (SCE). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in the frequency range of 10−2-105 Hz with a 20 mV amplitude sinusoidal disturbance. The recorded EIS data were fitted with Zsimpwin software to obtain detailed corrosion parameters. In addition, the corrosion resistance of composite coating with an artificial defect was studied by local electrochemical workstation system (Versascan, AMETEK, USA).

The adsorption of CQDs and the four main components from orange juice on the surface of Q235 steel was studied by the Forcite module in Material Studio software. The subjects were the surface of carbon steel, 1 inhibitor molecule, and 400 water molecules. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations used a COMPASS force field and a typical NVT ensemble, with a time step of 1.0 fs and a simulation time of 500 ps. All metal atoms were fixed during the calculation, keeping the adsorbed molecules in free interaction with the metal surface. The interaction energy (E interact) of the CQDs and the Q235 steel substrate was calculated as follows [33,34]:
E interact = E tot－(E sub + E inh)

In this equation, E
tot was the total energy of the whole system, E sub was the energy of the carbon steel substrate and all of the H2O molecules, and E inh was the energy of the free corrosion-suppressing molecules. In addition, the binding energy (E binding) was a negative value of the interaction energy [35]:
E binding = -E interact

In Fig. 2(a), the black spots were CQDs synthesis by orange juice, and the size range of CQDs distributed at 2–5 nm. Furthermore, as shown in the HR-TEM images of Fig. 2(d), the CQDs displayed clear lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.18 nm, corresponding to the [102] lattice plane of graphite [31]. Compared to the smooth surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets in Fig. 2(e), the rough surface topography of CQDs@CNNs hybrids illustrated the substantial CQDs was grown on g-C3N4 nanosheets in Fig. 2(c). In the TEM images of Fig. 2(f), there were a clearer observation that many black spots were grafted on g-C3N4 nanosheets. In the inset of Fig. 2(f), the lattice spacing of the CQDs@CNNs hybrids are consistent with the CQDs. In Fig. 2(g), the shape of CQDs was granular and relatively evenly dispersed. The longitudinal dimension of CQDs was mainly about 1 nm. The g-C3N4 nanosheets were uniformly distributed on the silicon substrate with a thickness of about 3 nm in Fig. 2(h). As shown in Fig. 2(i), after grafting by CQDs, some faint bright spots can be seen on the surface of the g-C3N4 nanosheets, suggesting that the CQDs are distributed on the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The presence of the CQDs would enhance the interface compatibility between g-C3N4 with epoxy resin. Scanning probe microscopy testing can further explicit the surface morphology of CQDs, g-C3N4 nanosheets, and CQDs@CNNs hybrids. However, the height of the CQDs@CNNs hybrids did not change significantly because the size CQDs was too small to affect the thickness of g-C3N4 nanosheets.

To probe into the dispersion of the prepared CQDs@CNNs hybrids, we observed the dispersion state of g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs@CNNs hybrids in aqueous solution, respectively. Standing for 7 d, the pure g-C3N4 nanosheets had been mostly precipitated, while the CQDs@CNNs hybrids still showed good stability as well as the inset of Fig. 3(a). As shown in Fig. 3(a), the absolute Zeta potential value (23.1 mV) of the CQDs@CNNs solution was significantly higher than that of the g-C3N4 nanosheets solution (10.7 mV). The larger the absolute value of the Zeta potential was, the better the stability of the solution was [36]. This result indicated that the CQDs containing a great deal of functional groups can promote the dispersion of the g-C3N4 nanosheets in the aqueous solution.

The FT-IR spectra of the CQDs@CNNs hybrids were shown in Fig. 3(b), pure g-C3N4 and CQDs@CNNs hybrids displayed similar FT-IR curves. The range of 3000–3400 cm−1 represented the vibration peak of the N-H/O-H group [37], whereas the O-H group was due to the uncondensed ammonia group on the surface and the adsorbed hydroxyl [38]. The characteristic peaks at intervals between 1800 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to the unique skeleton vibrations of the g-C3N4 aromatic ring [39]. The spike at 809 cm−1 was considered the tensile vibration peak of the g-C3N4 triazine ring [40]. After grafted by CQDs, a redshift of the g-C3N4 nanosheets occurred at the hydroxyl and amino peaks of 3000–3400 cm−1, which indicated some interfacial interaction between g-C3N4 and CQDs. From the UV spectral in Fig. 3(c), the modification of CQDs reduced the number of peaks from 3 to 2. In contrast to g-C3N4 nanosheets, this peak showed a redshift from 312 nm to 337 nm, implying the existence of π-π interaction between the CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets [41].

The XPS was used to analyze the element status of the CQDs, CNNs, and CQDs@CNNs hybrids. In Fig. 3(d), we observed that CQDs contained three elements of C, N and O. Meanwhile, the g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs@CNNs hybrids were mainly composed of elements C, N, and O. In Fig. 3(b) and Fig. 3(c), the XPS C1s and N1s spectra confirmed that the surfaces of the CQDs had numerous carbon-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and epoxy groups [42]. It should be noted that the presence of little oxygen was detected in g-C3N4 after treatment by strong alkali stripping. As shown by Fig. 3(e), the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of the raw g-C3N4 can be fitted by three peaks, including 284.6 eV of the C-C/CC, 286.0 eV of CO, and 288.1 eV of the N aromatic ring (N-CN) [13]. It should be noted that the C, O content (48.5%, 8.5%) of CQDs@CNNs hybrids was much greater than that of g-C3N4 nanosheets (43.8%, 5.0%), which further confirmed that CQDs were successfully grown on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The N 1 s spectrum was divided into three peaks at 398.6, 399.7, and 400.9 eV in Fig. 3(f), which represented sp2 N (C-N = C), bridging N (N-C)3, and N-H [43], respectively. There was no significant change for the intensity of the different N-containing groups, indicating that the skeleton structure of g-C3N4 nanosheets was still retained after the modification of the CQDs. Interestingly, the binding energy of the C 1 s was blue-shifted after introducing the CQDs, revealing the presence of an electron transfer between the CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets [44]. The maximum displacement of the C-O species was 0.3 eV, indicating that the CQDs were grafted onto g-C3N4 by the C-O bonding [41]. Besides the π-π interaction between g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs, XPS results also suggested that chemical interaction was formed, namely C-O bonding.

It is well known that the protective performance of the coating was closely related to the dispersion of the fillers in the polymer matrix [45]. In order to observe the dispersion of CQDs@CNNs hybrids in the composite coating, the cross section of the coating was characterized by SEM technology. As shown in Fig. 4(a), there are more microcracks in the pure EP coating, which will expand rapidly without any obstacle and easily cause the infiltration and rapid expansion of corrosive species. On the contrary, when g-C3N4 nanosheets were incorporated into epoxy resin, the cross-section morphology was rougher in Fig. 4(b), and the water contact angle increased to 71.2 。 in comparison to pure EP coating (58.7。 ). When CQDs@CNNs were incorporated into epoxy matrix, the surface of CQDs@CNNs/EP coating was more uniform, and there were almost no defects in Fig. 4(c). Due to the existence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs (O-CO, C-N, N = C, N-H), the covalent bonds, hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces can be formed between CQDs and the epoxy matrix containing the functional groups (C-O-C, O-CO) [25,46]. The CQDs attached to the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets play a bridging role, resulting in better interfacial compatibility between epoxy resin and g-C3N4 nanosheets. At the same time, CQDs detached from the surface of CQDs@CNNs could fill the cracks and tiny holes among epoxy polymer matrix, thus enhancing the compactness and integrity of epoxy coating. Furthermore, the contact angles of CQCN0.25/EP coating and CQCN0.5/EP coating were 87.2 ° and 83.8 ° in Fig. 4(e) ， respectively. It was proved that the incorporated CQDs@CNNs caused the change of the chemical state of the coating surface. The coating with higher contact angle had better barrier property to corrosive species, further demonstrating that CQDs can effectively improve the barrier properties of the coatings [47].

The adhesion strength between composite coating and Q235 steel substrate was one of the key indexes to evaluate the corrosion protection of coating. As shown in Fig. 4(f), CQDs@CNNs nanosheets can improve the adhesion of epoxy coating on Q235 carbon steel under atmospheric or wet conditions (immersion in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 7 days). The adhesion force of the pure EP coating under dry and wet conditions is 4.21 and 2.9 MPa, respectively. The dry and wet adhesion strength of CNNs/EP was 4.38 MPa and 3.1 MPa, respectively. For the CQCN0.25/EP, the adhesion strength was 5.75 MPa (dry) and 3.9 MPa (wet), respectively. Compared with CNNs/EP, the adhesion strength of CQCN0.25/EP was increased by 23.8% (dry) and 20.5% (wet), respectively, these results suggested the CQDs@CNNs could effectively prevent the peeling off of coating from the steel substrate, and thus endowed the composite coating with outstanding protective capability [48].

In the initial stage, all coatings were complete, free of defects, and no water penetration. All samples showed only one time constant in Bode phase diagram, which implied that the coating mainly played a barrier effect at this stage. The impedance result at low frequency (|Z|f=0.01 Hz) was a semi-quantitative index to evaluate the anti-corrosion performance of coating [49]. In the Nyquist diagram, the diameter of capacity arc reflected the difference of coating protection performance [50].

After immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 1 d, the impedance value of pure EP coating at the low frequency (|Z|f=0.01 Hz) was located at a low level (9.7 ×108 Ω cm2). With the extension of immersion time, the barrier effect of pure EP coating gradually weakened. In Fig. 5(a1), the two-time constant gradually appeared, indicating that water molecules and chloride ions had penetrated into the interface between the coating and metal substrate. Compared with pure EP coating, the CNNs/EP coating impedance value decreased slowly in Fig. 5(b), and basically were all maintained between 108 and 109 Ω cm2. This situation was due to the strong blocking effect of g-C3N4 nanosheets, which generated a maze effect in the coating, forming a barrier network and thus extending the diffusion path of the corrosive species. Due to the high surface energy, it was easy for g-C3N4 nanosheets to agglomerate in the epoxy polymer matrix, so g-C3N4 nanosheets were difficult to exert their barrier properties effectively. When the immersion time reached 35 d in Fig. 5(b1), the second time constant appeared for the CNNs/EP coating, and the impedance value at the low frequency region (|Z|f=0.01 Hz) decreased to 8.7 × 107 Ω cm2. As shown in the Fig. 5(b1), a clear platform was appeared at 25 d, then one new relaxation time also appeared within 10−2-100 Hz compared with that of 1 day, these result associated with the electrolyte-induced charge-transfer process at metal/coating interface [51]. The CQDs@CNNs/EP coating showed the higher impedance values compared with pure EP and CNNs/EP coating. The Bode phase diagrams of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coatings exhibited a stable capacitance response with nearly slope angle of 90 。 over a wide frequency range. In Bode modulus diagrams of Fig. 5(c) and Fig. 5(d), a high impedance modulus of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coatings excessed over 109 Ω cm2 at low frequency region (|Z|f=0.01 Hz). Moreover, in the Nyquist diagram of Fig. 5(c2) and Fig. 5(d2), this wide capacitive arc implied that CQCN0.25 /EP and CQCN0.5 /EP coatings showing excellent shielding performance and integrity. It was worth noted that the low-frequency impedance values (|Z|f=0.01 Hz) of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coatings were maintained between 109-1010 Ω cm2, which was thought to be that CQDs facilitated the dispersion and interface compatibility between g-C3N4 nanosheets and the polymer matrix. During a 20-day soaking procedure, the low-frequency impedance values (|Z|f=0.01 Hz) of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coating occurred a significant advancement. It has been attributed to the passivation of CQDs for metal substrate and the interactions of CQDs with the polymer matrix which exert an effect of inhibiting coating’s corrosion.

In order to further analyze the anti-corrosion behavior of the coating, the EIS data was fitted through the equivalent circuit model established by Zsimpwin software [52]. As shown in Fig. 6(a). Rs was solution resistance, Rc was the resistance to current flow through the pores in the coating, and it was inversely proportional to the porosity of the coating and proportional to the length of pores. Qc was constant phase element (CPE) of coatings. Rct was charge transfer resistance. Qdl was a double-layer capacitance between the coating/substrate interfaces, which relate to the water barrier property of the coatings. Rct and Qdl reflected the electrochemical corrosion process of substrate during immersion. During immersion, the solution reached carbon steel substrate through the permeable defects in coatings. There were diffusion impedances presented in EIS of epoxy coatings at later stage of immersion. The Warburg impedance (W) represents the process of the diffusion of ions from the bulk of the electrolyte to the interface between coating and metal substrate [51]. The part of the fitted data are correspondingly presented in the Table S1.

Due to a large number of cracks and holes, the corrosion species would penetrate into pure EP coating and CNNs/EP coating in a short time to reach the Q235 steel surface, resulting in electrochemical corrosion. Compared with CNNs/EP coatings, the R c value of pure EP coating decreased the fastest in Fig. 6(c) which was only 7.9 × 105 Ω cm2 after being immersed for 35 days. However, CQDs@CNNs/EP composite coatings showed relatively stable values throughout the immersion period. Especially for the CQCN0.25/EP coating, it had always shown the highest R c value, indicating its excellent barrier performance. R ct was related to the corrosion rate of the coating and showed a downward trend under normal circumstances [53]. After 35 days of immersion, the R ct value of CQCN0.25/EP coating still remained above 109 Ω cm2, which was 3 orders of magnitude higher than that of pure EP coating in Fig. 6(d). These results demonstrated that CQDs@CNNs hybrids can effectively prevent the entry of corrosive species and greatly enhance the protection ability of the coating.

Breakpoint frequency (f b) refers to the frequency when the phase angle was − 45 。 . It was considered an important parameter to evaluate the degree of coating damage and the detachment between the coating and the Q235 steel substrate. The f b value was closely related to the stripping area under the coating, and its enhancement reflects the separation degree of the coating from the Q235 steel surface [54]. The change of f b shows the opposite trend to R c and R ct in Fig. 6(e). With the extension of immersion time, the f b value gradually shifted to a higher frequency. For pure EP and CNNs/EP coating, the invasion of corrosive species caused the accumulation of corrosion products on the surface of Q235 steel, resulting in the failure of the coating. By adding CQDs@CNNs hybrids, the CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coating showed a lower f b value, which meant that the existence of CQDs can make g-C3N4 nanosheets give full play to their strong barrier performance, and prevent the invasion of corrosive species. Simultaneously, the rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs could be coupled with the Fe elements of the Q235 steel substrate, which could enhance the adhesion force between the coating and the Q235 steel substrate, and inhibit the detachment process of the coating [24,55].

After being immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 35 days, the corrosion products and morphology of the Q235 steel surface were characterized by SEM and SPM techniques. As presented in Fig. 7(a, b) and Fig. 7(d, e), more corrosion pits appeared on the surface of Q235 steel beneath EP and CNNs/EP coating. In their element distribution diagram, C and O elements were enriched in the black corrosion region, and the strength of Fe element was the weakest, which was deemed to the corrosion products. For CQCN0.25/EP coating, there were scarcely any black area in Fig. 7(g), and O, C and Fe elements were evenly distributed, indicating excellent corrosion protection performance. These results were attributed to the fact that the prepared nanofillers could fill the micropores and cracks of the coating and inhibit the invasion of corrosive species in the epoxy matrix. Laser confocal maps are an emerging strategy to characterize the surface roughness of metal substrate. As appeared in Fig. 7(c), the surface roughness of the Q235 steel under pure EP coating was about 2.033 µm due to the formation of numerous corrosion pits, which was the largest among all samples. This result was consistent with the corrosion morphology. After being protected by CNNs/EP coating, the surface roughness of Q235 steel was reduced to 1.989 µm due to strong barrier capacity of the g-C3N4 nanosheets in Fig. 7(f). By modifying with CQDs, g-C3N4 nanosheets can be more evenly dispersed in the CQCN0.25/EP coating, resulting in the lowest surface roughness of Q235 steel in Fig. 7(i).

The LEIS technology was susceptible to the local corrosion current and was an ideal tool to detect the active corrosion resistance of the coating [47]. The impedance value evolution characteristic of the coating defect was tested by LEIS technology to evaluate the ability to inhibit local corrosion. Artificial scratch with a width of 300 µm on the surface of coating was made for the sake of exposing the Q235 steel substrate, which will be used to analyze the corrosion reaction activity at the scratch. These defective coatings were immersed into the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, and the impedance values of the coating were recorded over time. In Fig. 8(a-l), the local-area electrochemical impedance centered on the damaged area was shown for the four samples (EP, CNNs/EP, CQCN0.25/EP, CQCN0.5/EP). After 24 h and 48 h of testing, the impedance values of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP samples in the damaged area did not decrease compared with pure EP coating. In order to make this LEIS data more intuitive, we summarized the key data from Fig. 8(a-l) to obtain Fig. 8(m, n), which included two parts: one was the central damage area without coating coverage and the other was the surrounding coverage area of coating. Fig. 8(m) showed the average values of the impedance modes at the center of the damage area with the lowest impedance, Fig. 8(n) taken on the average value of the angular impedance mold value of the coating with the highest impedance. The two values can reflect the corrosion status of the damaged area and the coating coverage area respectively. The impedance value of the damage center of EP and CNNs/EP samples decreased significantly, and the decline trend of the impedance value slowed down after 48 h, because the accumulation of corrosion products slightly hindered the contact between the corrosion medium and the metal substrate. However, the impedance value of CNNs/EP samples was 2 times higher than that of pure epoxy, due to the excellent barrier effect of the high transverse and longitudinal ratio of g-C3N4 nanosheets. The impedance values of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP samples in the central damaged area increased by about 1 order of magnitude than those of EP samples after 24 h, which was due to that a large number of CQDs adsorbed on the surface of the Q235 steel to form a passive film, reducing the local electrochemical activity of Q235 steel [56]. Due to a large number of corrosive media pouring into the damaged area of the coating, after 48 h, the corrosion reaction on the carbon steel surface was further intensified. The inhibited corrosion effect of the CQDs was not enough to resist the corrosion process of the coating, and the impedance value at the damaged area of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP samples began to decline slowly. It was worth noting that the impedance value of the damaged area was still lower than the area covered by the coating, indicating that the composite coating has a certain self-repairing ability, but not enough to completely inhibit the corrosion reaction of the damaged area.

For the area covered by the coating, as was shown in Fig. 8(n), the impedance value of EP sample showed a significant downward trend within 48 h, due to the penetration and diffusion of corrosion media among the coating. Compared with pure EP, the impedance of CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coating increased by about 1 order of magnitude. First of all, it was due to the adsorption of CQDs on the surface of the metal substrate, which exerts its externally passive self-repairing effect. Secondly, the covalent and hydrogen bond interactions between functional groups of CQDs (O-CO, C-N, N = C, N-H) and polymer chains of epoxy resin, as well as van der Waals forces of CQDs molecules, promoted the intrinsic actively self-repairing of epoxy coatings [25,46]. Notably, the existence of abundant groups on the surface of the CQDs, also gave rise to the improvement of the dispersion and interface compatibility of g-C3N4 nanosheets among the epoxy resin matrix, which elucidated that there was a synergistic role between CQDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets in hindering corrosion reaction. The physical barrier advantage of g-C3N4 nanosheets to the corrosive medium was fully utilized.

The corrosion inhibition performance of CQDs was evaluated using polarization curves. The Q235 steel was immersed 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 0.5 mg/mL CQDs, and analyzed corrosion behaviour of Q235 steel. The relevant corrosion parameters, including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), tafel slope (β a and β c) of anode and cathode, as well as corrosion inhibition efficiency (η), which were recorded in the Table S1. The value of corrosion efficiency was calculated from the following equation:

represented the current density of the blank sample and the control sample, respectively. Obviously, the addition of CQDs led to the decrease of corrosion current density from 3.83 × 10−6 μA cm−2 to 1.20 × 10−6 μA cm−2, and the corrosion potential shifted significantly from − 0.913 V to − 0.808 V. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 9(a), the existence of CQDs in the CQDs@CNNs hybrids made the corrosion potential also shift positively and decreased the corrosion current in comparison to Q235 steel. This corrosion inhibition phenomenon was attributed that CQDs could be adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel substrate, which coupled with metal ions to produce coordination compounds so that limit the corrosion rate of corrosive species to Q235 steel [24]. In order to verify the coupling effect of CQDs on the surface of Q235 steel, we observed a small amount coupling product on the surface of Q235 steel by way of FT-IR test. In Fig. 9(b), the Q235 steel embodied all characteristic peaks of CQDs, but an obvious change was observed in comparison to the FT-IR curve of CQDs, the intensity of the characteristic peak of CQDs abated significantly, further manifesting that CQDs formed a stable coordination compound with metal ions on the Q235 steel surface, which can be used as an effective corrosion inhibitor [47].

The purpose of molecular dynamics simulations was by way of predicting the most stable adsorption configuration and corresponding binding strength between CQDs and Q235 steel substrate, so as to explain the related corrosion inhibition mechanism [57]. In this process, four kinds of material molecules, namely, glucose, ascorbic acid, fructuose and amino acids, which are the main components of orange juice [58]. Five kinds of substances were compared to excavate the depth information about the interaction between CQDs and Q235 steel substrate. The stable adsorption configuration of these molecules and CQDs on the surface of Q235 steel were shown in Fig. 9(c-g). Compared with other molecules, it can be observed in Fig. 9(c) that CQDs molecules were almost parallel adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel. It meant that the formation of coordination bonds was accompanied between CQDs and Q235 steel. It was regarded that the parallel orientation of corrosion inhibitors can maximize the contact area between corrosion inhibitors and metal substrate, thus minimizing the surface area of metal substrate that can be eroded by corrosion medium. In order to get the results of a quantitative analysis, the binding energy was calculated between the corresponding Q235 steel and CQDs molecules, which was equal to 324.4 kcal/mol, it was higher than glucose (150.3 kcal/mol), ascorbic acid (129.6 kcal/mol), fructuose (136.3 kcal/mol) and amino acids (119.0 kcal/mol). The higher value of binding energy indicated that the corrosion inhibitor molecules were more strongly adsorbed on the surface of Q235 steel surface [35]. These results further confirmed that CQDs molecules could effectively inhibit the corrosion of Q235 steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

Self-monitoring performance was an important feature of intelligent anti-corrosion coating. This function was usually the ability of the material itself to convey damage or performance degradation through color change under auxiliary conditions [59,60]. The polymer coating generally does not have self-monitoring characteristics, and need to add functional filler to endow it the feature [61].

In Fig. 10(a), the aqueous solution of the CQDs displayed orange color under natural light, it showed fluorescent green color after UV irradiation exposure. The bright fluorescent color of the CQDs from orange juice originated from their inherent carbon core of band gaps and extrinsic surface defects [62]. It was marvellous for appearing the fluorescence quenching of CQDs, which was ascribed to the interaction between Fe3+ and oxygen-containing functional groups on CQDs [26]. This process further validated the excellent sensitive fluorescence response properties of CQDs.

To fully explore the self-monitoring properties of the composite coatings, as a quantitative analysis, the fluorescence intensity of epoxy polymer coating (EP, CNNs/EP, CQCN0.25/EP, CQCN0.5/EP) was characterized at λ ex= 405 nm the excitation light [63]. In Fig. 10(b), the fluorescence intensity of polymer coatings was observed ranged from small to large in the order: EP < CNNs/EP < CQCN0.25/EP < CQCN0.5/EP coating, the result implied that the higher the nanofiller content was, the stronger the fluorescence of coatings was. Ultra-thin g-C3N4 nanosheets have been previously reported to exhibit photo-luminescence, which served as nanofillers, to be observed to raise the fluorescence properties of the EP coating slightly. However, CQDs possessing a wealth of functional groups densely distributed on the g-C3N4 nanosheet surface, which made the g-C3N4 nanosheets better disperse among the polymer coating, and the small size of CQDs was easier to fill the cracks and holes of the coating, which can significantly heighten the fluorescence intensity of g-C3N4 nanosheets and the epoxy resin polymer.

The fluorescence properties of these coatings were investigated by biotype laser confocal microscopy testing (LEICA) under the UV light of λ ex= 405 nm, which was used as a qualitative analysis. All coatings were applied to the slide glass, which were cured for 2 d at room temperature and were processed by liquid nitrogen, in order to be easier to create invisible microscopic cracks for the naked eye. From Fig. 10(c), it was observed that the pure EP coating had a weak fluorescence, due to the interaction between the benzene rings among the epoxy polymer, but it was still difficult to observe the microcracks on the surface of EP coating. In Fig. 9(d), g-C3N4 nanosheets appeared as fluorescent green spots with irregular shapes in the epoxy coating, but still did not change the overall fluorescence intensity of the coating, and microcracks cannot be clearly observed. For CQCN0.25/EP and CQCN0.5/EP coatings, CQDs were grafted on the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, which enhanced the fluorescence intensity of composite coating to show bright irregular fluorescence sheets. Simultaneously, microcracks (12.3 µm) on the surface of coating were clearly observed in Fig. 9(e1). Notably, as the CQDs@CNNs fillers increased, the CQCN0.5/EP coating showed stronger fluorescence, facilitating the observation of microcracks on the surface of coating (16.7 µm) in Fig. 9(f1), which was consistent with the fluorescence spectrum of the coating.

During service, defects are like cracks, and holes are an indispensable part, which will lead to the immersion of corrosion ions, thus causing metal corrosion, the self-repairing mechanism of CQDs@CNNs/EP coating was vividly shown in Fig. 11. The rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs can not only improve the density of epoxy resin, but also improve the dispersion of g-C3N4 nanosheets in the epoxy resin matrix, which can give full play to the shielding performance of g-C3N4 nanosheets and extend the service life of the coating due to the labyrinth effect [49]. A part of CQDs detached from the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets, can couple with metal ions on Q235 steel, and adsorb on its surface, playing the role of external passively self-repairing [64]. Meanwhile, the covalent and hydrogen bond interactions between functional groups of CQDs and polymer chains of epoxy resin, as well as van der Waals forces of CQDs molecules, also promoted the intrinsic actively self-repairing of epoxy coatings [25,46]. Notably, CQDs had photo-luminescence properties, which endowed the coating with fluorescence characteristics [58]. Under ultraviolet light, the CQDs@CNNs/EP composite coating generated green fluorescence, which can be used to monitor the damage of the coating surface that the naked eye cannot observe.

In this work, water-soluble CQDs were prepared from orange juice. CQDs with a large number of oxygenated functional groups were grafted on the g-C3N4 nanosheets via hydrothermal method, improving the interface compatibility between g-C3N4 nanosheets and epoxy resin matrix. The small sized CQDs were easier to fill the tiny holes and gaps of the coating, thus improving the shielding performance of the coating, and made the coating show excellent anti-corrosion performance. After 35 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, the impedance modulus of the CQDs@CNNs/EP coating was increased by 3 orders of magnitude compared to the pure EP coating. More importantly, the water-soluble CQDs will be released from the coating to coordinate with the Fe3+ of the Q235 steel substrate when the coating was damaged, forming a passivation film in order to prevent the direct contact between the corrosive species and the Q235 steel surface, which realized the external passively self-repairing function for the damaged area in the coating. In addition, due to the covalent bond and hydrogen bond interactions between functional groups of CQDs and the polymer chain of epoxy resin, as well as the van der Waals force of the CQDs molecules, the epoxy resin coatings also exert an effect of intrinsic actively self-repairing. As a result of the photoluminescence behavior of CQDs, bright fluorescence of CCN/EP was observed by LEICA technology, using the fluorescence to detect microcracks invisible to the eye, realizing early monitoring of coating damage. This research provides a new idea for developing multi-functional (high impermeability, external passively self-repairing, intrinsic actively self-repairing and early corrosion monitoring) protective coatings.

Yiqian Lv: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wenjie Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yujie Qiang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jingmao Zhao: Visualization.
